title: Influence of the Buffer Organic Cation on the Chiral Separation of Some Basic Drugs by Capillary Zone Electrophoresis
abstract:
J. High Resol. Chromatogr. vol. 21, 445–449 (1998) No. 8
i WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69451 Weinheim 1998
0935-6304/98/0808–0445$17.50+.50/0
445
Influence of the Buffer Organic Cation on the Chiral Separation of
Some Basic Drugs by Capillary Zone Electrophoresis
YiYang Dong, Yiliang Sun*
Department of Chemistry, Peking University, Beijing 100871, P.R. China
Zengpei Sun
National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products, Beijing 100050, P.R. China
Ms received: February 24, 1998: accepted: June 2, 1998
Key Words: Chiral separation; organic cation effect; ethylamines; ethanolamines; basic drugs
Summary
This paper addresses the influence of small organic cations in the
background electrolyte (BGE) on chiral separation by capillary zone
electrophoresis (CZE). Seven basic drugs, viz. alprenolol, amphet-
amine, anisodamine, isoprenaline, pinacidil, synephrine, and verapa-
mil were used as test compounds with hydroxylpropyl-b-cyclodextrin
(HP-b-CD) as the chiral selector. Resolution of the chiral test com-
pounds was studied at pH 2.5 in the presence of Tris, ethanolamine,
diethanolamine, triethanolamine, diethylamine, and triethylamine,
which exist in their cationic forms at this pH value. Among the six
cationic additives studied, triethylamine is most effective in enhan-
cing resolution. A tentative mechanism of the effect is proposed.
1 Introduction
In the separation of chiral drugs by capillary zone electrophoresis
(CZE), the choice of a suitable chiral selector is of paramount
importance along with the selector concentration [1], the pH of
the background electrolyte (BGE) [2–4], and organic modi-
fier [5–7], etc. Among these, the effect of selector concentration
seems to have been well studied while the choice of selector and
the effect of organic modifiers appear somewhat unpredictable.
Thus the optimization of separation parameters has to be per-
formed largely by trial and error.
Up to the present, the effect of organic cations in the BGE has
not been extensively studied. The effect of inorganic cations has
been studied by Issaq et al. [8, 9], Green and Jorgenson [10],
Valko´ [11], Nielen [12], and Chiari et al. [13]. With regard to the
effect of organic cations, Valko´ [11] mentioned that Tris cations
in phosphate buffer had a favorable influence on the resolution
of chiral mandelic acids. Kuhn et al. [14] also reported the influ-
ence of Tris and triethylamine as buffer constituents on the
migration time and resolution of DL-tryptophan. Moreover,
some organic amines such as triethylamine [15], triethanolamine
[15], ethylenediamine [16], propylenediamine [17], butanedi-
amine [18], pentanediamine [19], etc., which exist in their catio-
nic forms at low pH, have been widely used as buffer additives
in CZE for suppressing the adsorption of basic proteins. In chiral
separations the use of organic amines is rarely mentioned [12].
In the separation of some basic chiral drugs, a phosphate buffer
is generally used at a pH value near the drug’s pKa1 to ensure that
the drug is positively charged and to protect the cationic analytes
against interactions with the capillary wall [20]. In the present
paper the effect of six organic cations, including two ethylamines
and three ethanolamines and Tris in phosphate buffer at pH 2.5,
on the chiral separation was investigated to see whether the
cationic effect can be employed to enhance the resolution.
2 Experimental
2.1 Apparatus
Experiments were carried out with a laboratory-assembled CE
system. Uncoated fused silica capillary of 52 cm length (effec-
tive length 35 cm) with 50 lm i.d. 6 375 lm o.d. (Yongnian
Optical Fiber Factory, Hebei, China) was used as a separation
tube. A laboratory-made high-voltage power supply that can pro-
vide voltage from 0 to 30 kV was used to drive the separation.
On-column detection was performed at the cathode with a CV4
UV detector (ISCO, Lincoln, NE, USA) at 210 nm with a rise
time of 0.8 s. Electropherograms were recorded on a SE 120
recorder (ABB Goerz Instruments, Vienna, Austria). A small fan
was used to dissipate the Joule heating generated by the power.
A pHs-3C pH meter with an E-201-C combination electrode
(Rex Instrument Factory, Shanghai, China) was used for pH mea-
surements.
2.2 Chemicals
Hydroxypropyl-b-cyclodextrin (HP-b-CD) was obtained from
Aldrich Chemical Company (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Tris-
(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) was from Fluka (Buchs,
Switzerland). Enantiomeric drugs alpranolol, amphetamine, ani-
sodamine, isoprenaline, pinacidil, synephrine, and verapamil
(structural formulae shown in Figure 1) were provided by
National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biologi-
cal Products (Beijing, China). All other chemicals were of analy-
tical grade. The water used was deionized and redistilled.
2.3 Preparation of Background Electrolyte (BGE) Solution
50 mM phosphoric acid was prepared by diluting 85% concen-
trated phosphoric acid with water and then adjusted to pH 2.5
with sodium hydroxide, Tris, ethanolamine, diethanolamine,
triethanolamine, diethylamine, or triethylamine, respectively.
HP-b-CD (207 mg, 15 mM) was dissolved in 10 mL buffer. The
solution was filtered through 0.45-lm membrane filters and
degassed by sonication prior to use.
2.4 Experimental Procedure
The sample solutions were prepared by dissolving each solute in
50 mM phosphoric acid to give an approximate concentration of
0.1 mg/mL so that adequate signals could be obtained. It was
found that the activation and the equilibration of a new column
 Dong, Sun, Sun
446
VOL. 21, AUGUST 1998
J. High Resol. Chromatogr.
is of utmost importance for obtaining reproducible migration
times, as already mentioned by Gozel et al. [21]. Preliminary
experiments with pinacidil as test analyte were performed to
obtain a good migration time reproducibility. The new column
was vacuum rinsed with 1 M NaOH overnight (A8 h) to activate
the silica wall and then equilibrated with deionized water and the
operating buffer for 30 min each. During operation the capillary
was rinsed with the operating buffer for 5 min between two con-
secutive runs. In this case, good reproducibility (RSD = 1.01%,
n = 10) was obtained.
Samples were injected by the electrokinetic method at the anode
and CZE operations were performed under constant voltage at
ambient temperature (about 30 8C).
The column efficiency N was calculated by the following equa-
tion:

respectively.
3 Results and Discussion
3.1 Separation Behaviors in the Buffer Added with Organic
Amines
Seven chiral drugs were used as test compounds to investigate
the cationic effect. By adding organic amines to the buffer solu-
tion in place of sodium hydroxide, the migration times of the test
drugs were increased to different extents with concurrently
decreasing electroosmotic flow. These phenomena were espe-
cially pronounced with the two ethylamines as buffer cations,
since the electroosmotic flow was suppressed so strikingly as to
reverse its direction, as seen in Table 1. Viscosity measurement
with a viscosimeter at 30 l 0.5 8C showed that there was no
noticeable variation of viscosity on addition of organic amines at
a concentration of about 40 mM into the running buffer. There-
fore, the migration time increase could only be explained by the
enhanced adsorption of the protonated ethanolamine or ethyl-
amine cations due to the electrostatic attraction between the
negatively charged surface and the amine cations
Compared with ethylamines, the ethanolamines and Tris are less
potent in suppressing EOF because of their inherently greater
hydrophilicity. Of the two ethylamines used, diethylamine
showed a stronger suppression of the electroosmotic flow than
did triethylamine, probably due to the lower steric hindrance of
diethylamine in interactions with the residual silanol anions on
the capillary wall.
The resolution of the chiral drugs in buffers with various cationic
species were calculated from the electropherograms obtained
and are illustrated in Figure 2.
It is interesting to note that the resolutions of the seven chiral
basic drugs were all improved on addition of these organic amine
cations to the running buffer, as exemplified by pinacidil and ani-
sodamine (see Figure 3 and Figure 4). Among the six amines
studied, triethylamine was most effective in improving the reso-
lution for all the seven drugs under investigation.
Figure 1. Structural formulae of seven test chiral drugs.
Figure 2. Resolution (Rs) of chiral drugs in buffers with various cationic
species.

Table 1. EOF and average migration times of the enantiomers (in seconds, n = 3).
The upper value represents the migration time of the earlier eluting enantiomer and the lower one the later eluting one.
Contains two pairs of enantiomers, the values denote the migration times of the first pair of enantiomers.
Figure 3. Enhanced chiral separation of pinacidil by organic cations.
Conditions: field, 385 V/cm; buffer, 50 mM phosphoric acid and 15 mM
HP-b-CD adjusted to pH 2.5 with (a) sodiium hydroxide; (b) Tris; (c)
ethanolamine; (d) diethanolamine; (e) triethanolamine; (f) diethylamine;
(g) triethylamine; capillary length, 52 cm (35 cm from anode to the
detector); capillary diameter, 50 m; temperature, ambient (308C); detec-
tion, 210 nm; injection, electrokinetic, 5 kV/8s.
Figure 4. Enhanced chiral separation of anisodamine by organic cations.
Conditions: field, 385 V/cm; buffer, 50 mM phosphoric acid and 15 mM
HP-b-CD adjusted to pH 2.5 with (a) sodium hydroxide; (b) Tris; (c)
ethanolamine; (d) diethanolamine; (e) triethanolamine; (f) diethylamine;
(g) triethylamine; capillary length, 52 cm (35 cm from anode to the
detector); capillary diameter, 50 m; temperature, ambient (308C); detec-
tion, 210 nm; injection, electrokinetic, 5 kV/8s.
According to Giddings [22] and Jorgenson and Lukacs [23],
resolution and selectivity in capillary zone electrophoresis are
correlated by the following equation:
where Rs is the resolution, N the column efficiency, Dlep the
mobility difference between the two analytes to be separated, lep
their average electrophoretic mobility, leo the electroosmotic
mobility of the system, and lep + leo the average apparent mobi-
lepleo is also called the selectivity term which
has a stronger influence on the resolution than the efficiency
[24]. As seen from our measurement of column effi-
ciency shown in Figure 5, the addition of these small organic
amines surprisingly did not increase the separation efficiency
which might result from the worsened peak shape especially
when triethylamine was added to the background electrolyte;
interestingly, the peak shape obtained when Tris and triethanol-
amine acted as cationic species seems unaffected which might be
more practically useful to enhance the chiral resolution later.
From eq. (4), by increasing Dlep and/or decreasing lep + leo one
can increase the selectivity term and thus improve the resolution.
Mobility difference and apparent average mobility were calcu-
lated and are shown in Figure 6 and Figure 7, respectively. The
slight fluctuation of the mobility difference (see Figure 6) could
not totally account for the improvement of separation. The
enhancement of resolution can only be reasonably explained by
the decrease of the average apparent mobility due to the presence
of the organic cations in buffer. From Figure 7 it is evident that
among all the amines tested, triethylamine was always most
effective in decreasing the denominator term of eq. (5), the aver-
3.3 Applicability of the Method
Unlike some other experimental parameters that may enhance
the resolution of a pair of analytes while impairing the separation
of others, the cationic effect herein could improve the resolutions
of all analytes simultaneously.
Compared with higher pH, the electroosmotic flow is consider-
ably suppressed at pH 2.5. This also applies when sodium hydro-
xide is used to adjust the buffer pH, as is commonly the case.
However, for those analyte pairs which are unresolvable in
sodium ion buffer, use of the organic amines mentioned above
instead of sodium ion can afford a new variable for further
enhancing the resolution. After tediously optimizing the chiral
selector and its concentration for anisodamine enantiomers,
Wang et al. [25] reported a near baseline resolution within
19 min for the earlier eluting pair of enantiomers (Rs L 1.0) and
a slightly inferior resolution for its later eluting pair (Rs L 0.8)
with 100 mM Tris-H3PO4 (pH 2.3) containing 12 mM HP-b-CD
and a capillary of 62 cm total length (41 cm to the detector). In
the present study, the two enantiomeric pairs of anisodamine
were baseline separated with enhanced resolution (1.4 for the
first pair and 1.2 for the second) within 16 min with a shorter
capillary of 52 cm total length (35 cm to the detector).
The organic cationic effect seems to be of potential use in pep-
tide mapping, amino acid analysis, etc., where simultaneous
improvement of the resolution of all analytes is required.
Figure 5. Column efficiency (N) of chiral drugs (in 105 m – 1) in various
Figure 6. Mobility difference Dlep (in 10 – 6 cm2 V – 1s – 1) of the enantio-
Figure 7. Average apparent mobility (lep + leo) (in 10 – 4 cm2 V – 1s – 1) of
chiral drugs in various buffers.
Influence of the Buffer Organic Cation on Chiral Separation by CZE
The chiral separation of seven basic drugs, i.e., pinacidil,
synephrine, isoprenaline, alprenolol, anisodamine, amphetamine,
and verapamil, has been investigated with the addition of small
organic amines as buffer additives in CZE. The improvement of
chiral resolution was accomplished by decreasing the average
apparent mobility of the analytes.
The financial support jointly provided by the Doctoral Program Founda-
tion of the State Commission of Education and National Natural Science
Foundation of China are gratefully acknowledged.
